title: Honeymoon

jokes.text: This was a good choice. / We make good choices. / This was a really healthy choice.
jokes.setup: Two people waking up together after an unexpected one-night stand, clearly in an awkward post-hookup moment
jokes.punchline: They unconvincingly reassure each other with increasingly hollow affirmations that this was 'healthy'
jokes.explanation: A strong cold open. The escalating affirmations ('good choice' → 'really healthy choice') perfectly capture the post-hookup denial energy. Works because both characters are performing confidence they clearly don't feel. Character-specific — David and Stevie's particular brand of ironic deflection.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That was a good choice.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Really good choice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Very good choice.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Excellent choice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Best choice we've ever made.

jokes.text: He saw me. Fuck! It's the towels again, we need towels!
jokes.setup: Johnny Rose has spotted Stevie in the hallway outside David's room; David and Stevie panic
jokes.punchline: Stevie's emergency cover story is the hilariously flimsy 'it's the towels again' — a transparent lie that escalates the cringe
jokes.explanation: The panic-improv of 'it's the towels again' is funny because it's so transparently bad as a cover story. The word 'again' adds an extra layer — as if towel emergencies are routine. Classic cringe escalation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh my god, oh my god, oh my god. We need to get out of here before anyone sees us.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David, relax. No one saw anything.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No one saw anything? Stevie, I am literally standing here in a towel that says 'Property of the Rosebud Motel' on it.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Okay, but that's not even one of our nice towels.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: That's your concern right now?

jokes.text: Your dad can't know about this. It's humiliating for me.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are trying to hide their hookup from Johnny
jokes.punchline: David frames the humiliation as being specifically 'for me' — a hilariously self-centered take on a mutually awkward situation
jokes.explanation: The 'for me' is the key word — David's narcissistic self-focus is perfectly in character. It's a tiny moment of character comedy that lands because it reveals exactly who David is without belaboring the point.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Do you have any towels?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, we have some old t-shirts and a bath mat from 2003.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That's not... those aren't towels.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Well, they absorb water, so technically...

jokes.text: Stevie offering a face cloth and a bath mat as 'towels'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Stevie for towels; she's flustered and trying to get him to leave
jokes.punchline: She produces a face cloth and a bath mat — the most inadequate possible 'towels' — and presents them as if this is acceptable
jokes.explanation: A good physical/visual escalation of the cringe. The specificity of 'face cloth and bath mat' is funnier than just 'some towels.' Stevie's desperation to end the interaction is palpable.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Hello? Is someone in there?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm going to need you to come out slowly. Keep your hands where I can see them.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We can work through this. Whatever you're feeling right now, I understand.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, it's just the cat.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, please. I'm in the middle of a very delicate negotiation.

jokes.text: Are you in danger? Blink, if you're in danger.
jokes.setup: Johnny is suspicious that something is wrong with Stevie behind the door; she's acting strangely
jokes.punchline: His concern escalates to the absurd hostage-negotiation tactic of 'blink if you're in danger' — treating a towel transaction like a crisis intervention
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. Johnny's fatherly concern getting misdirected into a hostage-rescue scenario is perfectly absurd, and the specific instruction 'blink if you're in danger' is both highly quotable and character-perfect — Johnny is earnest and slightly clueless. The economy is excellent: four words ('blink if you're in danger') do enormous comedic work.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I... I see. Well. That's... that's very... I'm just going to... yes. I'll just be... somewhere else. Anywhere else. Right now.

jokes.text: Dad, please, just take the towels and go!
jokes.setup: Johnny is hovering suspiciously outside the door; David is trying desperately to end the scene
jokes.punchline: David breaks and just yells at his father through the door — the cover blown, the desperation complete
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the towel sequence. Solid cringe escalation but not especially inventive on its own — works as a button to a good sequence rather than a standout joke.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, David, so you're finally getting back out there! I'm so excited for you!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: It's really not a big deal. He's just someone I met. It probably won't go anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: That's so hot! When are you seeing him again?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm not sure we will be. He seemed very interested in cryptocurrency.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my God, that's amazing! You have so much in common!

jokes.text: Oh. Well, I, uh... I've got everything I need. Good seeing you both.
jokes.setup: Johnny has clearly figured out that David and Stevie slept together; the situation is now unambiguous
jokes.punchline: Johnny reacts with almost comical composure — 'I've got everything I need' — treating the revelation like a business transaction and politely exiting
jokes.explanation: Johnny's deadpan 'I've got everything I need' is a beautifully controlled reaction. It works on multiple levels: the literal (he has his towels), the figurative (he's pieced together the situation), and the character level (Johnny would absolutely say this). The 'Good seeing you both' is the cherry — polite, slightly wounded, deeply funny.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Two weeks? That's serious.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Alexis, I've been with Patrick for two years.

jokes.text: You slept with Stevie. / What, did Dad tell the whole motel? / Well, no, but I heard through the wall, 'cause he was doing his high-pitched voice.
jokes.setup: Alexis confronts David about sleeping with Stevie; David assumes Johnny told everyone
jokes.punchline: Alexis reveals she heard it herself through the wall because Johnny uses a distinctive 'high-pitched voice' — a specific, absurd family detail
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Johnny has a specific 'high-pitched voice' he uses in distress is a wonderful piece of character-building comedy. It implies a whole history. The setup (David assuming Dad told everyone) and the reality (Alexis just heard it naturally) is a nice misdirection.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: An unplanned pregnancy is simply a contraceptive failure manifesting as a nine-month inconvenience with a lifetime of financial and emotional ramifications.

jokes.text: So, was it cute for you, or what? / It was fine. It was... It was weird. It was good. / Love that. / It was good but weird. / I love that!
jokes.setup: Alexis pries for details about David's hookup with Stevie
jokes.punchline: David's fumbling 'fine... weird... good' contradictions and Alexis's gleeful 'I love that!' response perfectly captures their sibling dynamic
jokes.explanation: Alexis's repeated 'I love that!' is perfectly calibrated — excited, slightly mocking, entirely self-interested. David's contradictory self-assessment is very him. The rhythm of the exchange is well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Well, I'm not pregnant, so I guess I'm not a 'plague of locusts descending upon the family.'

jokes.text: Because a podunk baby out of wedlock is not something your father and I can afford to negotiate at this moment.
jokes.setup: Moira is warning her adult children to be careful with their hearts regarding their local relationships
jokes.punchline: Her concern escalates to the specific nightmare scenario of 'a podunk baby out of wedlock' — framed primarily as a logistical inconvenience
jokes.explanation: Moira's ability to make everything about logistics and inconvenience is peak character comedy. 'Podunk baby out of wedlock' is a highly specific phrase only Moira would use, and 'can't afford to negotiate' frames parenthood as a corporate liability. Classic Moira.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm just going to say it. You guys need to get a life.

jokes.text: Well, that's crazy, because all I want is some podunk baby out of wedlock!
jokes.setup: Moira has just expressed horror at the idea of a 'podunk baby out of wedlock'
jokes.punchline: David immediately echoes the phrase back sarcastically, declaring it his life's dream
jokes.explanation: Immediate callback that works because David's deadpan delivery of Moira's own absurd phrase is perfect. The echo structure makes it feel inevitable once you hear the setup.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, like last week, we went to the grocery store and then we came home and David microwaved some salmon.

jokes.text: Uh, walking over to the cafe and ordering a tuna melt is hardly what I would call a 'flourishing social life.'
jokes.setup: Moira has described her social life as 'flourishing'; David interrupts to correct the record
jokes.punchline: The specific detail of 'ordering a tuna melt' as the entirety of Moira's social activity perfectly deflates her self-regard
jokes.explanation: The tuna melt is the perfect specific detail. It's not generic ('going to a cafe') — it's the exact banality of 'a tuna melt' that makes it funny. David as the deflator of Moira's grandiosity is core to their dynamic.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom, Dad, you can't just—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: You know what? I suddenly have no idea what we were talking about.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Mm, same. Completely blank.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You literally just walked in here—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Nope. Gone.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Vanished. Like my first marriage.

jokes.text: David and Alexis tell their parents they need to 'get a life' and feel 'suffocated' — then both parents immediately forget what they came to say.
jokes.setup: The kids have just turned the tables on their parents, lecturing them about being too involved.
jokes.punchline: Both parents, clearly rattled, admit they can no longer remember what they originally came to discuss — their authority completely deflated.
jokes.explanation: A well-structured scene reversal — parents come to lecture, leave lectured. The mutual forgetting at the end is a tight button on the scene. The structure earns a high score because the payoff is properly built.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, you know, David has always been a bit ambiguous.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: He's not ambiguous. He's gay.

jokes.text: ...and that was fine when we were child actors
jokes.setup: Alexis is explaining to her parents that their over-involvement was fine when the kids were young
jokes.punchline: The casual reveal that both David and Alexis were apparently 'child actors' — a completely unhinged backstory detail delivered as if it's unremarkable
jokes.explanation: The offhand 'when we were child actors' is a brilliant piece of Rose family lore dropped with zero fanfare. The joke is entirely in the casualness — Alexis says it like it's obvious and unremarkable. This kind of incidental world-building comedy is Schitt's Creek at its best.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: So what was that exactly?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I don't know, what do you want to call it?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it wasn't... I mean, we didn't...

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Right, so it was just a...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: A blop?

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Did you just say 'blop'?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know what we're doing here, Stevie.

jokes.text: Well, now I don't remember. / Yeah, I don't remember.
jokes.setup: Moira and Johnny came to talk to their children about something specific; the children have hijacked the conversation
jokes.punchline: Both parents have been so thoroughly derailed that they've forgotten what they came to say — delivered with total bewilderment
jokes.explanation: A solid button to the family confrontation scene. The parallel 'I don't remember / Yeah, I don't remember' captures how completely the children have won the argument without trying. Good character work.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, I'm going to go... skooch myself to the store. Very skooch. Much skooch energy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Don't wait up. I'll be back when I'm back. Or whenever. Time is a flat circle of skooch.

jokes.text: You know, the more I think about it, the more I wonder whether David's whole sexually adventurous thing was just a phase.
jokes.setup: Moira and Johnny are processing the children's conversation about being suffocated
jokes.punchline: Moira pivots to casually theorizing about David's sexuality — the 'sexually adventurous thing' framing is both dismissive and hilariously euphemistic
jokes.explanation: Moira's breezy 'sexually adventurous thing' to describe David's bisexuality/pansexuality is perfectly Moira — she's not malicious, just grandly obtuse. Sets up the more developed pansexual conversation later.

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: So what would you consider cheating in a relationship?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: Because I think if you're using someone else's hiking trail without asking, that's basically cheating.

jokes.text: It's not a phase. / Well, you know, he was very influenced... all those haircuts. / It's not a phase, John.
jokes.setup: Moira has suggested David's sexuality might be a phase; Johnny appears to offer a theory about influence
jokes.punchline: The haircut theory — whatever its exact content — is so absurd that Moira shuts it down with 'It's not a phase, John,' which is funnier for being the same refrain
jokes.explanation: The haircut theory is gloriously opaque — we don't hear the full thing, which makes it funnier. Moira becoming the voice of reason by repeating 'It's not a phase' (which she just said herself) is a nice irony.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: It's not a barbecue, it's a soiree.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Roland, there are hot dogs on a card table.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Yes, well, a sophisticated card table.

jokes.text: Jonathan and I were just saying, wouldn't it be fun to have the mayor for cocktails and charades tonight! / Yes, tonight.
jokes.setup: Moira has just invited herself and Johnny to Jocelyn and Roland's evening after specifically not being invited
jokes.punchline: Moira presents the invite as if it were the Schitts' idea all along, and Johnny helpfully confirms 'Yes, tonight' — the social brazenness played completely straight
jokes.explanation: Moira's social maneuvering is classic — she makes it sound like she's doing them a favor by inviting herself. 'Cocktails and charades' is very Moira. Johnny's confirming 'Yes, tonight' shows he's learned to just go along.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: We're having a soiree.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: A what?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: A soiree. It's French for a fancy evening party.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, we can't have a soiree. We've got a pig hanging in the garage.

jokes.text: We're having a huge party tonight. Ton of people coming to our house. / Big. / But we're free any other night other than tonight.
jokes.setup: Moira has just invited herself to the Schitts' party; they clearly don't want the Roses there
jokes.punchline: Their desperate insistence that the party is 'huge' and they're very busy — obviously overclaiming to avoid the invitation — is seen through immediately by anyone watching
jokes.explanation: The overclaim backfires in a predictable but well-executed way. Roland's 'Big' single-word confirmation is the best beat. Not groundbreaking but serves the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Well, we just... we didn't think you'd want to come.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: You're not really our people.

jokes.text: That was just a... / Was a one-time thing. / Just a blip... / Just a blip. Blop. Just a...
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are trying to verbally process and dismiss their hookup in the motel lobby
jokes.punchline: The shared attempt to finish each other's sentences collapses into 'blip... blop... blippity blop' — language itself failing them
jokes.explanation: The 'blip/blop/blippity blop' evolution is genuinely inventive. The joke is that they're so awkward they can't even complete the cliché 'one-time thing' dismissal. It's character-specific (both David and Stevie would handle it exactly this way) and musically funny in its nonsense syllables.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm like a wine that's hard to pair. Some people think I'm too dry, others find me too sweet, and most people just get a headache.

jokes.text: You're quiet today. / Am I? / It's just that you're not talking, and it's annoying, because I'm bored.
jokes.setup: Alexis and Mutt are doing community service together; Mutt is quiet
jokes.punchline: Alexis's reason for wanting him to talk — 'it's annoying, because I'm bored' — is hilariously self-centered; she's not concerned about him, just her own entertainment
jokes.explanation: 'It's annoying, because I'm bored' is perfectly Alexis — she has no interest in how he's actually doing, she just needs stimulus. Efficient character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I did, actually. I cried myself to sleep, and then I woke up at 3 a.m. in a panic, and then I cried myself back to sleep.

jokes.text: You know that if it's in your dreams, or over the clothes, it's not cheating, right?
jokes.setup: Mutt has admitted he's been having feelings for someone while in a relationship with Twyla
jokes.punchline: Alexis offers her confident, completely made-up rules for what does and doesn't constitute cheating — delivered as gospel truth
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. The 'dream cheating' loophole is funny enough, but 'over the clothes' as a moral threshold delivered with absolute authority is hilarious. It reveals Alexis's entire ethical universe — she has clearly lived by these rules. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific, and economically perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: This is the 'Scandinavian minimalist' collection. It's actually just a bed frame and a nightstand from a big box store, but it sounds better when you say it that way.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: And this one here is the 'mid-century modern restoration' — it's particle board, but the legs are angled, which apparently makes all the difference.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: My personal favorite is the 'contemporary farmhouse aesthetic.' It's literally just wood painted white with some metal handles. Cost me forty dollars.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, what kind of event are you hosting this evening? / Event. / Oh, it's more of a soiree.
jokes.setup: Roland has described his party; Moira has heard enough to be appalled
jokes.punchline: Moira corrects his 'event' with 'soiree' — an absurd upgrade that reveals her inability to engage with anything on its own terms
jokes.explanation: Moira's word substitution is a micro-joke about class pretension. 'Soiree' for Roland's pig roast is a funny mismatch. Not exceptional but functional Moira comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Everyone, I just want to say that these rolls are fresh from the microwave!

jokes.text: We have a big one hanging in the garage.
jokes.setup: Roland confirms they roast pigs at their parties; Moira and Johnny are processing this
jokes.punchline: The casual 'we have a big one hanging in the garage' — delivered as normally as one would say 'we have beer in the fridge' — is the full realization of the culture clash
jokes.explanation: The economy of this line is great — 'a big one hanging in the garage' conjures a vivid, slightly disturbing image with perfect nonchalance. Roland's utter lack of self-awareness about how this sounds to the Roses is the comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're like the perfect waitress.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I mean, you're so good at it, you could actually be a waitress.

jokes.text: So whose pig do we have to slaughter to get an invite?
jokes.setup: Moira has realized they weren't initially invited to the pig roast
jokes.punchline: She riffs on the pig theme with 'whose pig do we have to slaughter' — a play on 'whose baby do we have to kiss' — delivered with theatrical indignation
jokes.explanation: A genuinely good line. Moira's riff on 'whose baby do we have to kiss' refashioned as pig-slaughter is the right level of absurd wordplay for her character. The specificity ('slaughter' rather than just 'roast') adds a touch of darkness that lands.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: That suit is absolutely gorgeous. I just want to touch it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, thank you. It's a Tom Ford.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: No, I mean I want to touch *you* in it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh! Well, that's... that's not appropriate.

jokes.text: Hoity-toity. All right, I've said it.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn is trying to explain why the Roses weren't invited; she's been dancing around the real reason
jokes.punchline: 'Hoity-toity' is the word she lands on — antiquated, slightly embarrassed, but said with the relief of someone who finally got it out
jokes.explanation: 'All right, I've said it' is the perfect addendum — Jocelyn has been working up to 'hoity-toity' as if it's a devastating accusation. The word choice itself (very specific, slightly old-fashioned) is funny, and her relief at saying it is funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Well, I smoke a little marijuana. What are you gonna do, arrest me? I'm the town council.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: I'm also the law. So if I arrest myself, that's an abuse of power by me against me.

jokes.text: Regular people are how Rose Video became such a... / Right, ca-careful, Moira. / Regular people! I mean, they made us... / What am I trying to say? / We love regular people. / We love regular people.
jokes.setup: Moira is trying to argue they love regular people; she keeps almost saying something damning
jokes.punchline: Moira's repeated near-gaffes while Johnny nervously coaches her — culminating in the hollow 'we love regular people' — is perfectly performed class-obliviousness comedy
jokes.explanation: Johnny's nervous 'ca-careful, Moira' is wonderful — he knows exactly what she was about to say. The fact that both of them land on the same rote 'we love regular people' makes it feel like a rehearsed PR statement they've clearly used before.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Go ahead, Rollie! Don't be shy!

jokes.text: So, just to be clear, um, I'm a red wine drinker. / That's fine. / But, uh, I only drink red wine. / Okay. / And up until last night, I was under the impression that you too only drank red wine. / But I guess I was wrong?
jokes.setup: David is trying to process Stevie's sexuality using an elaborate wine metaphor
jokes.punchline: The dawning realization that he's been monologuing in wine metaphors and she's just been politely letting him — until the penny drops for both of them
jokes.explanation: The beginning of one of the show's most celebrated exchanges. David's wine metaphor for sexuality is clever because it reveals his own assumptions — he thought Stevie was straight, like him (red wine only), and is gently processing that she's not. The structure of building up the metaphor before letting Stevie respond is well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Oh, I have a very close relationship with the FDA. My dermatologist's cousin works there. So I'm comfortable with my choices.

jokes.text: I do drink red wine. But I also drink white wine. And I've been known... And a couple summers back I tried a Merlot, that used to be a Chardonnay. / Which got a bit complicated. / Oh. Okay. / Yeah, so, you're just really open to all wines. / I like the wine and not the label.
jokes.setup: Stevie uses David's wine metaphor to explain her own sexuality/openness
jokes.punchline: 'A Merlot that used to be a Chardonnay' — a wine metaphor for a trans partner — delivered with complete sincerity; and David's final response 'I like the wine and not the label' being his own identity crystallized in the metaphor
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the entire series — and genuinely one of the best bits of comedy writing in modern sitcom television. The wine metaphor does extraordinary work: it's funny, it's touching, it encapsulates both characters' sexualities with perfect precision, and 'I like the wine and not the label' became a cultural touchstone for pansexuality representation. The 'Merlot that used to be a Chardonnay' is the peak — it's absurd, it's warm, and it's a genuinely novel way to address trans identity in a comedy. Every craft score is justified: original concept, perfect structure (the metaphor builds and pays off), absolutely character-specific (only David would do this), highly economical, and entirely earned.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Washing a car. Yes, that's a real punishment. I'm sure they're devastated. Next week, perhaps they'll be forced to organize a closet. The week after that, maybe fold some linens. Truly, the justice system has spoken.

jokes.text: No, no, no, I absolutely did that. Just wept for hours in the dark.
jokes.setup: David asks if Stevie cried herself to sleep with regret after their hookup
jokes.punchline: Stevie deadpans complete confirmation — 'absolutely did that, just wept for hours in the dark' — completely straight-faced
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in deadpan. The joke is that David set up his own reassurance ('you didn't cry yourself to sleep, right?') and Stevie immediately destroys it with total agreement. 'Just wept for hours in the dark' is perfectly excessive and perfectly delivered in Stevie's flat affect. Highly quotable, character-defining moment.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Well, we just... talk about nothing really. Like, he'll say something about the weather, and I'll say something back about the weather, and then we'll just kind of stand there and look at each other for a while.

jokes.text: Wow, Ted, the place is very... new looking. / Did you design the interior? / Oh, I wish. No, I found this website online that sells off old department store showrooms. So this whole room... just gets delivered, as is. This is the 'bachelor's den' here, and then that's the 'executive dining lounge.'
jokes.setup: David and Stevie arrive at Ted's apartment for the party
jokes.punchline: Ted's entire apartment is furnished with entire department store display rooms delivered wholesale — complete with showroom names like 'bachelor's den' and 'executive dining lounge'
jokes.explanation: Ted's showroom apartment is a genuinely original piece of character comedy. The specific detail that the rooms have official display names ('executive dining lounge') that Ted has apparently just adopted wholesale is hilarious. It tells you everything about Ted: earnest, slightly oblivious about aesthetics, very sincere. David's 'very... new looking' diplomatic opener is perfect too.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: Well, thank you. I actually ordered it all online from a restaurant supply company.

dialogue.speaker: Ted
dialogue.line: It's a tax write-off if it's for business purposes.

jokes.text: Ted explains his entire apartment was bought from a website selling department store showrooms — it comes 'delivered, as is,' including 'the bachelor's den' and 'the executive dining lounge.'
jokes.setup: David asks about Ted's interior design.
jokes.punchline: Ted reveals the rooms came pre-named from the department store — 'bachelor's den' and 'executive dining lounge' — as if these are perfectly normal room designations.
jokes.explanation: The department store showroom apartment is a brilliantly conceived visual/absurdist gag. Ted's earnest acceptance of 'bachelor's den' and 'executive dining lounge' as real room names is perfect character comedy — he's not embarrassed, he's proud. The concept rewards rewatching because once you know, you see it everywhere in the scene.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: What if I had become a professional yodeler in the Swiss Alps? I'd have three ex-husbands, all named Klaus, and a pet chamois named Mr. Whiskers who was also my accountant.

jokes.text: So are we the only ones showing up to this party? / No, we're still waiting for Mutt and Twyla. / Oh, I should take the rolls out of the microwave. Hot rolls!
jokes.setup: David realizes the 'party' is extremely small; Ted confirms it's just a few people
jokes.punchline: Ted's pivot to 'Hot rolls!' — complete with exclamation point energy — while David processes that he's been misled about the party's scale
jokes.explanation: Ted's enthusiastic 'Hot rolls!' as a subject-change while David absorbs the party-size revelation is well-executed character comedy. Ted's earnestness is always funny when it's tone-deaf to someone else's disappointment.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I met Roland at a... well, it was a real estate seminar, and he had these eyebrows that just... they came into the room before he did.

jokes.text: David, Stevie, and Alexis's reaction shots to Ted's apartment interior.
jokes.setup: Ted has just explained the apartment was a delivered showroom set.
jokes.punchline: The three visitors share loaded 'Wow... yeah... wow' reactions — a symphony of polite horror.
jokes.explanation: Standard reaction beat — functional rather than exceptional. Works as a grace note to the apartment joke.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Was it funny?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: What? No. I mean, yes. I mean, not in a bad way. I was laughing because I was happy, not because anything was actually... I wasn't laughing at you.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm just saying, the laughing was contextual. It was a happy laugh. A satisfied laugh.

jokes.text: Alexis had just said that this was a 'party' party. / Ah, well, you must've been misinformed then, David.
jokes.setup: David expected a large party based on Alexis's description
jokes.punchline: Ted's cheerfully diplomatic 'you must've been misinformed' — putting the blame on David without acknowledging any deception on his own part
jokes.explanation: Ted's 'you must've been misinformed' is gentle but effective. The polite blame-deflection is character-perfect for a guy who would never acknowledge he orchestrated a small dinner party and called it a party.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm not funny. I'm not trying to be funny. What you're describing sounds like something that would happen to Twyla's friend who charges by the hour.

jokes.text: Twyla, I love your outfit, you look like...
jokes.setup: Stevie greets Twyla with a compliment that she doesn't complete — the sentence trails off
jokes.punchline: The unfinished compliment implies whatever she was going to say was not flattering enough to complete — the silence IS the joke
jokes.explanation: A beautifully Stevie moment — she starts to pay a compliment, clearly can't think of anything good enough (or thinks of something too honest), and just stops. The trailing-off is character-perfect for someone who doesn't bother with social niceties.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David's gay.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh, David's gay? That's wonderful! So he's got one of those fancy European kitchens?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: No, Roland, he's homosexual.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Oh! A homo... sexual... is that like a home sexual? Does he like having sex at home?

jokes.text: So, where is everybody? / It is. It's an exclusive little party.
jokes.setup: Mutt arrives to discover the 'party' is five people
jokes.punchline: Ted reframes four guests as 'exclusive' — a magnificent piece of positive spin
jokes.explanation: 'It's an exclusive little party' as a response to 'where is everybody?' is a nice deadpan. Ted's refusal to acknowledge the obvious is well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, I think what you need to do is just pick one gender and stick with it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, I'm not confused about my gender.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, then what's the problem?

jokes.text: Hey, who turned off Don Ho? / Aloha, Roses! / It's the Schitts! / Yeah, there you go.
jokes.setup: Roland and Jocelyn arrive at the pig roast and greet the Roses
jokes.punchline: Johnny's resigned 'Yeah, there you go' to being greeted as 'the Schitts' — a callback to the name joke, delivered with practiced weariness
jokes.explanation: Johnny's 'Yeah, there you go' as his acknowledgment of the Schitt name is well-worn but executed with exactly the right resigned energy. Not a fresh joke but well-maintained running gag.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: It's like watching a therapy session on reality TV, except everyone involved is actually terrible at communicating, and there's a good chance someone's going to cry into their wine.

jokes.text: The Schitts arrive at the Ted party — 'Aloha, Roses!' and Roland's Hawaiian shirt energy crashing what was a quiet dinner
jokes.setup: A small, awkward dinner party is already underway
jokes.punchline: Roland and Jocelyn burst in with full Hawaiian pig-roast energy — 'Aloha, Roses!' — completely wrong for the vibe of the evening
jokes.explanation: The collision of Roland's exuberance with the quiet dinner scene is a reliable comedic contrast. His 'Aloha, Roses!' as a greeting is funny for the sheer inappropriateness of tone.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, Stevie, I have to say, you're taking this remarkably well. I mean, for someone of your... limited acting range, this is quite the performance.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Thank you?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: In fact, I'd say you're Emmy-worthy. Outstanding Guest Actress in a Short Form Comedy or Drama Series worthy.

jokes.text: I just wanna wipe my hands all over that! / Oh! / You just keep getting funnier. / I'm not joking though, I really do.
jokes.setup: Roland compliments Johnny's suit by saying he wants to 'wipe his hands all over it'
jokes.punchline: When Johnny laughs it off as a joke, Roland insists — 'I'm not joking though, I really do' — making the weird even weirder
jokes.explanation: Roland's insistence that he genuinely wants to rub his hands on Johnny's suit is peak Roland — the joke is that there's no social filter, no self-awareness that this is weird. 'I'm not joking though, I really do' is the perfect escalation. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, I want you to know that I'm very supportive of your... lifestyle. Very supportive. I've been reading about it.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, you don't need to read about it.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I want to understand. I'm very open-minded. Very progressive. You know, some of my best friends are... well, they're not, but they could be.

jokes.text: Moira, I have to ask you. Are those wigs real hair? I just wanna reach out and just touch them... / No, Maureen does not like to be manhandled!
jokes.setup: Roland is fascinated by Moira's wig and wants to touch it
jokes.punchline: Moira refers to the wig in the third person as 'Maureen' — the wig has a name and a personality and does not consent to being touched
jokes.explanation: Moira naming her wigs and speaking for them in the third person is an absolutely inspired character detail — this is the first time we (presumably) hear 'Maureen' named. 'Does not like to be manhandled' adds Moira's theatrical indignation on the wig's behalf. Highly quotable, deeply original, and perfectly character-integrated. This is the kind of throwaway joke that reveals an entire inner world.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not drunk, I'm high. And let me tell you something about reproduction — it's overrated. Don't do it.

jokes.text: Moira: 'No, Maureen does not like to be manhandled!' — about her wig, when Jocelyn tries to touch it.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn asks if Moira's wigs are real hair and reaches out to touch them.
jokes.punchline: Moira has named her wig 'Maureen' and defends its personal space with the same dignity she'd use for a person.
jokes.explanation: A near-perfect Moira moment. The wig having a name ('Maureen') implies a whole relationship. 'Does not like to be manhandled' is perfectly regal and defensive. This is character comedy at its finest — Moira's eccentricities feel consistent and lived-in. Could only be Moira. Justified 8 on craft — comparable to the calibration anchors in terms of character specificity and quotability.

jokes.text: If I tell you a secret, you promise to keep it? / Uh, no, I don't think we would.
jokes.setup: Roland is about to reveal something confidential
jokes.punchline: Johnny's immediate, flat 'no, I don't think we would' — refusing to make a promise he can't keep — is a refreshingly honest break from the social script
jokes.explanation: Johnny's refusal to make a social-nicety promise is a perfectly delivered subversion. Most people say 'of course' reflexively — Johnny's honest 'no, I don't think we would' is unexpected, character-true, and very funny. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: I'm the guy in charge of upholding [the law], so if you have any ideas about turning me in, you'd just be turning me in to myself.
jokes.setup: Roland is about to share something illegal (smoking marijuana) and reassures them there's no legal consequence
jokes.punchline: His logic that reporting him would just be him reporting himself — because he's the mayor — is a self-serving but internally consistent piece of small-town corruption comedy
jokes.explanation: Roland's 'you'd just be turning me in to myself' is a funny encapsulation of small-town authority — the mayor IS the law, and he knows it. The circular logic is delivered without a hint of embarrassment. Good Roland character work.

jokes.text: I don't know if you guys wanna partake in this, because I'm sure your fancy dinner parties didn't have illegal substances. / Illegal substances. / Amateurs. / We had friends in the FDA, but... / When in Rome...
jokes.setup: Roland offers marijuana and assumes the Roses won't partake given their sophistication
jokes.punchline: Moira and Johnny's 'Amateurs / We had friends in the FDA' reveal that they were far more debauched than Roland assumes — the Roses out-Rolands Roland
jokes.explanation: The subversion of expectations is perfect — Roland assumes sophistication = abstinence, but it's the opposite. 'We had friends in the FDA' is a beautiful specific detail (they didn't just do drugs; they were connected enough to have regulatory friends). 'Amateurs' as Moira's one-word verdict is her at her most imperious. Strong character moment.

jokes.text: Well, damn, Roses! / Let this party begin. / I'll fire up the cheese fountain.
jokes.setup: The Roses have impressed Roland with their drug history; he declares the party truly started
jokes.punchline: Johnny's 'I'll fire up the cheese fountain' — delivered as the natural, normal next step — is the absurd capstone. A cheese fountain at a pig roast is Roland's idea of luxury.
jokes.explanation: 'I'll fire up the cheese fountain' is a deeply funny line because a cheese fountain is both extravagant and deeply un-extravagant. It's Roland's version of upscale entertainment delivered with total sincerity. 'Fire up' as the verb adds to the mechanical absurdity.

jokes.text: Mutt, did you two criminals do any good for... / We washed a car. / You washed a car. Wow. / The judicial system is really cracking down on its delinquents these days, huh?
jokes.setup: David asks what Mutt and Alexis did for their community service
jokes.punchline: Car washing is the entirety of their punishment; David's sarcastic 'the judicial system is really cracking down' nails the absurdity
jokes.explanation: David's sarcasm is well-deployed here. 'You washed a car. Wow.' with the pause before 'Wow' is perfect deadpan. The 'judicial system really cracking down on its delinquents' is a solid ironic observation.

jokes.text: What do you two talk about all day out there? / Nothing. / I mean, we talk, but... We don't really talk about... / So, like, what? / Like, nothing.
jokes.setup: David probes what Alexis and Mutt talk about during community service
jokes.punchline: Mutt and Twyla's confirmation that they talk about 'nothing' — a relationship so different from David's world that he can't even process it
jokes.explanation: More thematic than comedic — the joke is the contrast between Mutt's laconic self-sufficiency and David's verbal world. Works as character contrast but isn't especially funny on its own.

jokes.text: Ted, this is delicious. / Thank you, Stevie. I ordered it online / No sales tax.
jokes.setup: Stevie compliments the food at Ted's dinner party
jokes.punchline: Ted reveals the dinner was ordered online — and his highlight is 'no sales tax' rather than the quality of the food
jokes.explanation: Ted's enthusiasm for the 'no sales tax' detail is perfectly him — he's found a bargain and that's what he's proud of. Very specific character comedy. Pairs well with the showroom apartment reveal.

jokes.text: So when the lasagna gets delivered... Does it come, uh...
jokes.setup: David is processing that even the food at this dinner party was delivered wholesale
jokes.punchline: He can't even finish the question — the implication of what he was going to ask (does it come in the pan? pre-plated?) is funnier for being unsaid
jokes.explanation: David's trailing question works as a reaction beat to Ted's apartment/food situation. Not a standout but contributes to the running Ted-is-oblivious comedy.

jokes.text: It's very fresh and new. / No, it's not. / It is, I would say. / Mm-mmm.
jokes.setup: Someone has asked how long David and Stevie's relationship is; David claims it's 'fresh and new'
jokes.punchline: Stevie immediately contradicts him — 'No, it's not' — implying they've known each other much longer than the hookup suggests
jokes.explanation: The disagreement over whether their dynamic is 'new' is a nice small moment — Stevie's immediate flat contradiction suggests a comfort/familiarity that predates the sex.

jokes.text: Sorry, I thought everyone knew. / No. / It's okay.
jokes.setup: Ted has apparently just outed David and Stevie's relationship to the dinner party
jokes.punchline: Ted's innocent 'I thought everyone knew' — not realizing he's caused embarrassment — is sweet cringe
jokes.explanation: Ted's oblivious 'I thought everyone knew' is mild cringe. Not a strong joke but functions as a beat in the awkward dinner party sequence.

jokes.text: I've been with these people before. I know them all. / Wow, you've really been getting around town. / No, I was with them in my other life.
jokes.setup: Moira, at the pig roast, says she knows these people — implying she's slept with them (getting around town)
jokes.punchline: She clarifies 'my other life' — meaning her previous celebrity life, not a sexual history in Schitt's Creek
jokes.explanation: Nice misdirection — the 'getting around town' reading is briefly plausible before 'other life' recontextualizes it. Moira's casual reference to 'my other life' as a separate existence is very her.

jokes.text: Jocelyn confesses to Moira that she sometimes wonders 'what if' — her alternatives including 'what if Roland hadn't been such a smooth talker'
jokes.setup: Moira asks if Jocelyn has never dreamed of 'a life beyond'
jokes.punchline: 'What if Roland hadn't been such a smooth talker' — the joke is that Roland, as we know him, being described as a 'smooth talker' is the most implausible thing imaginable
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the audience's knowledge of Roland — calling him a 'smooth talker' is completely absurd given everything we've seen. This is a good piece of dramatic irony played for laughs.

jokes.text: What if I had enlisted in the military, what if Roland hadn't been such a smooth talker, what if I had not been crowned... and moved to the city with my driving instructor?
jokes.setup: Moira asks Jocelyn if she has 'what ifs' about a life beyond this town
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's 'what ifs' escalate from plausible (military) to deeply specific and strange ('smooth talker' Roland, being 'crowned,' moving to the city with a driving instructor)
jokes.explanation: Jocelyn's 'what if' list is great character work — the escalation from normal to 'moved to the city with my driving instructor' implies a whole romance we'll never see. 'Roland hadn't been such a smooth talker' also recontextualizes Roland as a seducer, which is its own joke.

jokes.text: What if I had not been crowned... and moved to the city with my driving instructor? / Awesome. / And what if I had not hand modeled at that microwave trade show?
jokes.setup: Both women are sharing 'what if' life scenarios
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'what ifs' include being crowned (beauty queen? something else?), running away with a driving instructor, AND hand modeling at a microwave trade show — each more specific and surreal than the last
jokes.explanation: The 'microwave trade show' hand modeling is a genuinely inspired piece of Moira backstory. The specificity of 'microwave trade show' (not just any trade show) is absurdist in exactly the right way — it implies this was a step up in her career at the time. The escalation of increasingly specific glamorous-but-not-really regrets is perfect Moira. This line earns its 8+ through sheer character-specific originality.

jokes.text: What if I didn't ask, 'Who's the eyebrows buying everyone's drinks?'
jokes.setup: Moira continues her 'what if' reverie about how she met Johnny
jokes.punchline: 'Who's the eyebrows buying everyone's drinks?' — Moira's first impression of Johnny was entirely about his eyebrows, and she asked about them publicly
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest lines. 'Who's the eyebrows buying everyone's drinks?' is an extraordinary piece of writing — the framing of a person by only their most distinctive feature ('the eyebrows'), the question structure that implies she asked this out loud, and the whole thing being Moira's origin story with Johnny. Highly quotable, perfectly economical, deeply character-specific. This is the kind of line that defines a character.

jokes.text: Wow, now you know. / Whatever happened to privacy?
jokes.setup: Moira has just revealed publicly how she and Johnny met ('the eyebrows')
jokes.punchline: Johnny's deflated 'Whatever happened to privacy?' — embarrassed but not surprised — is a perfect reaction
jokes.explanation: Johnny's weary reaction is good but secondary to Moira's line. 'Whatever happened to privacy?' is a nice touch — he's been Moira's husband long enough to have this complaint ready to go.

jokes.text: Now your whole family thinks I'm this big hooker. / My whole family does not think... / It was a funny one-night thing. / Everyone here is fine with it. / It was funny?
jokes.setup: Stevie is upset that the dinner party apparently discussed her hookup with David
jokes.punchline: David calls it 'funny' — meaning lighthearted — which Stevie hears as a dismissal of the experience, escalating the awkwardness
jokes.explanation: The miscommunication of 'funny' is relatable cringe — David means it affectionately but Stevie hears it as diminishing. The setup for Twyla's interjection.

jokes.text: I had a best friend that hooked. She married one... / Wow! So cool. / It's not the time, Twyla.
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are in the middle of an argument about the hookup; Twyla interjects
jokes.punchline: Twyla has a story about a friend who 'hooked' (apparently as a sex worker?) and married a client — delivered as helpful and positive — and David has to shut her down
jokes.explanation: Twyla's oblivious non-sequitur is perfect for her character — she always has an inappropriately cheerful anecdote. 'She married one' is the killer detail. David's 'It's not the time, Twyla' is the right straight-man response. Good Twyla character moment.

jokes.text: Twyla, non-sequitur: 'I had a best friend that hooked. She married one of her clients. Wow! So cool.' — apropos of the word 'hooker.'
jokes.setup: Stevie has used the word 'hooker' in a self-deprecating context.
jokes.punchline: Twyla immediately shares a completely sincere anecdote about her friend who was an actual sex worker and married a client — 'Wow! So cool.' with full enthusiasm.
jokes.explanation: Classic Twyla — she takes a metaphorical word literally and shares a completely sincere, inappropriate anecdote from her life. The 'Wow! So cool.' capper is perfect Twyla voice: non-judgmental, enthusiastic, entirely wrong-footed socially. This could only be Twyla. The joke works on multiple levels: it deflates the tension, reveals character, and the escalation to 'married one of her clients' is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Um, Stevie, I don't think you're a big hooker. / You don't talk all dinner, and now you're telling me I can't talk about Trixie?
jokes.setup: Ted tries to reassure Stevie; Twyla is offended she can't tell her Trixie story
jokes.punchline: Twyla's complaint that she was quiet all dinner and now she's being silenced — about 'Trixie' (presumably the friend who 'hooked') — is a great escalation of her character's offbeat grievances
jokes.explanation: Twyla naming the friend 'Trixie' is the escalation that makes this land. The name choice is perfect — obviously a sex worker name in context. Twyla's aggrieved defense of her right to discuss Trixie is excellent character comedy.

jokes.text: Is there a right time to talk about being a hooker? / I bet nobody thought sex with Trixie was funny.
jokes.setup: The table has tried to shut down Twyla's Trixie anecdote
jokes.punchline: Twyla continues with 'I bet nobody thought sex with Trixie was funny' — defending both her friend and the seriousness of sex work with complete sincerity and total social obliviousness
jokes.explanation: A truly great Twyla line. 'I bet nobody thought sex with Trixie was funny' is so earnestly delivered and so completely inappropriate to the moment that it transcends cringe into something almost poetic. Twyla is defending her friend's professional dignity while accidentally destroying any remaining dinner party decorum. High quotability, perfect character integration.

jokes.text: We were washing a car and talking about bad dreams. / Okay? / Okay. / You sound a little defensive. / Well... / I'd agree.
jokes.setup: Ted has been pressing Alexis and Mutt about what they were talking about during community service; Alexis finally snaps
jokes.punchline: Alexis's 'We were washing a car and talking about bad dreams' is meant to be definitive; Ted's calm 'You sound a little defensive' and Mutt's 'I'd agree' are perfect double-team deflation
jokes.explanation: Mutt's laconic 'I'd agree' is the best beat — he's barely involved in the drama but delivers a perfect one-word verdict. Ted's calm observation does the same. Good ensemble comedy.

jokes.text: My son lives in a barn in the woods, by choice.
jokes.setup: Roland is apparently discussing his son Mutt with Johnny at the pig roast (post-dinner departure of the younger group)
jokes.punchline: 'By choice' — the two-word addition that makes this a joke. Without it, it's just information. With it, it becomes Roland's barely-suppressed bafflement at his son's life decisions
jokes.explanation: Two words — 'by choice' — doing extraordinary comedic work. The economy is near-perfect. Roland's entire relationship with his son's bohemian choices is captured in this brief aside. It's devastating and funny and reveals something real about Roland beneath the buffoonery. 'In a barn in the woods' is already good; 'by choice' is what makes it a great line.

jokes.text: He could be the next mayor of this town...
jokes.setup: Roland is lamenting his son Mutt's choices; he gestures toward Mutt's potential
jokes.punchline: The implication that 'next mayor of this town' is the pinnacle of ambition — and the irony that being mayor of Schitt's Creek is not a high bar — is gentle but real
jokes.explanation: Mild irony — 'next mayor of Schitt's Creek' as a wasted destiny is funny but understated. Works as part of the Roland/Mutt characterization.

jokes.text: My son is pansexual. / Mm-hmm. / I've heard of that. / I know what that is, that's, uh, that cookware fetish. / No. / No, no. / No, he loves everyone. Men, women, women who become men, men who become women.
jokes.setup: Johnny reveals his son is pansexual; Roland confidently says he knows what that is
jokes.punchline: Roland's definition — 'that cookware fetish' — is an incredible malapropism (pan → pans → cookware), followed by Johnny's earnest, patient correction
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. Roland's 'cookware fetish' is a perfect piece of wordplay-through-ignorance — pan/pansexual → pans → cookware — delivered with total confidence. What elevates it further is that the scene doesn't mock the concept of pansexuality; it mocks Roland's confident ignorance. Johnny's patient correction ('Men, women, women who become men, men who become women') is warm and earnest. The combination of brilliant malapropism + warm-hearted follow-up is exactly Schitt's Creek at its best.

jokes.text: I'm his father, and I always wanted... But, you know, just pick one gender, / Maybe everything would've been less... / Confusing.
jokes.setup: Johnny is confiding in Roland about David's pansexuality and his own mild confusion about it
jokes.punchline: Johnny's trailing 'just pick one gender' and Roland supplying 'confusing' — Johnny's loving but slightly baffled parenthood is captured here without judgment
jokes.explanation: A warm, slightly awkward character moment. Johnny's 'just pick one gender' is funny because it's so earnestly clueless rather than malicious — he's not against it, he just finds it logistically confusing. Roland completing his sentence with 'confusing' is a nice bonding moment between two fathers.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'No. No, no. No, he loves everyone. Men, women, women who become men, men who become women.' — correcting Roland's pansexuality definition.
jokes.setup: Roland has just called pansexuality a cookware fetish.
jokes.punchline: Johnny's earnest, thorough definition of his son's sexuality — 'women who become men, men who become women' — delivered with paternal matter-of-factness, making it both funny and touching.
jokes.explanation: Johnny's correction is simultaneously funny and genuinely warm — he's been listening, he understands, and he explains it the way you'd explain something to a child. The repetition structure ('men... women... women who become men... men who become women') has a mild comedic rhythm. But it's mostly touching, which is Schitt's Creek at its best.

jokes.text: We can't tell our kids who to love. / Who said that? / You did. / When? / You just said it now! / When? / Now, right this second. You just said it. / And you're right. You can't tell them.
jokes.setup: Roland delivers an unexpectedly wise observation; Johnny asks who said it, as if it's a famous quote
jokes.punchline: Johnny's repeated confused 'When?' — unable to accept that Roland just said something wise — and Roland's patient 'Right this second' / 'And you're right' is a beautiful moment of comedic role reversal
jokes.explanation: A standout joke. The structure — Roland says something wise, Johnny can't accept it came from Roland, and keeps asking 'When?' — is brilliantly constructed. The repetition of 'When?' escalates perfectly. It's funny because it reveals Johnny's low opinion of Roland while simultaneously making Roland the wiser one. The warmth underneath makes it resonate beyond the laugh.

jokes.text: Thank you for playing along. / I'm sorry, I just had... / It was like being in an episode of Couples Therapy.
jokes.setup: Moira has apparently been having an earnest emotional conversation with Jocelyn that was, she now reveals, a put-on to escape dinner
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn compares it to being 'in an episode of Couples Therapy' — validating how strange it was while also being funny about it
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke of 'Couples Therapy' works modestly. The reveal that Moira was performing the whole conversation is the bigger laugh; this is the button.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'And you're right. You can't tell them.' — immediately accepting wisdom he just stated and forgot.
jokes.setup: Roland has convinced Johnny that Johnny's own quote was wise.
jokes.punchline: Johnny accepts this secondhand wisdom from Roland — wisdom he himself just uttered — with complete earnestness.
jokes.explanation: A good button on the 'when?' sequence. Johnny's stoned acceptance of his own wisdom as newly received is a nice grace note.

jokes.text: This was all a put-on just to get out of dinner? / Yeah, I thought you were in on it. / Well, you deserve a Daytime Emmy... / Thank you, I think so.
jokes.setup: Jocelyn discovers Moira was performing the emotional conversation to escape the dinner party
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's sarcastically delivered 'you deserve a Daytime Emmy' lands because Moira immediately and sincerely accepts it — 'Thank you, I think so'
jokes.explanation: The joke lives in the mismatch: Jocelyn means it sarcastically, Moira accepts it as a sincere award nomination. 'Thank you, I think so' is perfect — Moira genuinely believes she deserves a Daytime Emmy for getting out of a dinner party. Deeply character-integrated and well-structured.

jokes.text: I think it's fair to say that neither of us have ever worked... / True. / It was funny. / And a little humiliating. / Yeah, I, uh... Saw a lot of my body. I didn't like it. So...
jokes.setup: David and Stevie are debrief-walking after the dinner party disaster; they're reflecting on the hookup
jokes.punchline: Stevie's 'I saw a lot of my body. I didn't like it.' — a completely unexpected, deeply honest, hilarious post-hookup reflection on self-image
jokes.explanation: A genuinely great line. 'I saw a lot of my body. I didn't like it.' is simultaneously funny and vulnerable — Stevie's deadpan delivery of a real insecurity as throwaway self-criticism is peak Stevie. The period at the end of 'So...' implies she's genuinely concluded something about herself and moved on. Highly quotable, deeply character-specific, and economically perfect.

jokes.text: Try it again, for the sake of practice? / I'd be into that idea. / For the sake of proficiency.
jokes.setup: David suggests they try the hookup again; both are trying to frame it as practical rather than emotional
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'practice' to 'proficiency' — both using the same clinical framing to avoid admitting they actually like each other
jokes.explanation: The 'practice/proficiency' escalation is sweet and funny — the clinical vocabulary is their shared defense mechanism against vulnerability. 'For the sake of proficiency' is Stevie one-upping the awkward formality while completely agreeing to the idea. Very character-true for both.

jokes.text: So I guess we won't be playing Hedbanz.
jokes.setup: Ted had planned games for the dinner party; his guests have all left or dispersed
jokes.punchline: His resigned, perfectly timed 'I guess we won't be playing Hedbanz' — alone with the board game — is a picture of gentle, earnest disappointment
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best single lines. The specificity of 'Hedbanz' (not just 'games' — an actual branded children's/family guessing game) is perfect. It tells you exactly who Ted is: someone who planned a wholesome evening of Hedbanz with couples and had it completely derailed by drama. The deadpan, resigned delivery earns maximum economy points. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Oh! Why did he say that? / Oh, my God. / What the hell...
jokes.setup: Ted's Hedbanz line has apparently been overheard by David and Stevie who were just about to go hook up again
jokes.punchline: Their horrified reactions to Ted's comment (apparently interrupting their moment) set up the Johnny arrival cringe
jokes.explanation: Transitional beat more than a joke — sets up the Johnny arrival. The reactions are amusing but not particularly crafted.

jokes.text: Oh, Stevie, I hope you're being smart. / Oh, my God! / Because we can't afford a baby right now, and let's be honest, neither can you!
jokes.setup: Johnny is showing fatherly concern for David and Stevie's relationship
jokes.punchline: His contraception advice culminates in 'let's be honest, neither can you' — gently but directly pointing out that Stevie, working at a small-town motel, is also not in a position for parenthood
jokes.explanation: Johnny's accidental condescension toward Stevie — 'let's be honest, neither can you' — is very funny because he means well but is totally unaware of how it lands. The callback to the family's financial situation adds another layer. Johnny Rose as the world's most uncomfortably earnest father is peak character.

jokes.text: I just want you to know that I support this. Not the fact that you're sleeping with a girl, / But, you know, I'd be just as happy if you were to bring home one of your guy friends.
jokes.setup: Johnny is trying to express support for David's sexuality and relationship choices
jokes.punchline: Johnny's phrasing — 'not the fact that you're sleeping with a girl' (suggesting mild surprise at that choice) followed by 'just as happy if you bring home one of your guy friends' — inadvertently reveals more surprise at the heterosexual choice than the broader spectrum
jokes.explanation: A very warm, well-crafted moment. The joke is that Johnny's earnest attempt at inclusive support accidentally implies he's more surprised David's with a woman than he would be if David brought home a man. It's sweet, it's funny, and it's very Johnny — he's trying so hard and getting it slightly wrong in a harmless way. Good character integration.

jokes.text: David, I just want you to know that I endorse all your sexual encounters.
jokes.setup: Johnny is wrapping up his supportive speech to David about his relationship with Stevie
jokes.punchline: 'I endorse all your sexual encounters' — the boardroom language applied to parental sexual acceptance is both funny and deeply Johnny Rose
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest lines. 'I endorse all your sexual encounters' is extraordinary because of the word 'endorse' — a term from business/public relations applied to parenting. Johnny means it lovingly, but it sounds like a press release. Deeply character-specific (Johnny is a businessman trying to parent), highly quotable, and brilliantly economical. This is exactly what Johnny Rose would say.

jokes.text: Are you drunk? / Grass, too much grass. / A little bit, but...
jokes.setup: David is bewildered by his father's unusual warmth and openness
jokes.punchline: Johnny admits he's a bit high from the pig roast — 'grass, too much grass' — which retroactively explains his entire speech
jokes.explanation: A good callback to the marijuana at Roland's party — Johnny's uncharacteristic warmth is now explained. 'Grass, too much grass' is a funny phrase, very Johnny. The reveal recontextualizes the whole endorsement speech.

jokes.text: Hey, track that cycle, missy!
jokes.setup: Johnny is leaving after his 'endorsement' speech; he delivers a parting shot to Stevie about contraception
jokes.punchline: 'Track that cycle, missy!' — a high-pitched, stoned, borderline-unhinged contraception reminder delivered as he exits — is the perfect button to the scene
jokes.explanation: The episode's best button and one of its top jokes. 'Track that cycle, missy!' combines multiple layers of comedy: the archaic phrasing ('missy'), the practical birth control advice, the stoned energy of Johnny finally feeling loose, and the character-perfect delivery from a man who just endorsed all of David's sexual encounters. Highly quotable, unexpected, and perfectly timed. The exclamation point captures the delivery exactly.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira attend Roland’s annual Hawaiian bash. Alexis and Ted throw a couple’s dinner party, while David and Stevie’s relationship heats up.
insight: Season finale balances character depth with 41 jokes—their strongest cringe-irony blend yet.
